prompt:
How can I extract data from an HTML table and use Ruby to generate a summary of "The Benefits of Volunteering" article based on the Volunteer_Work table with columns Volunteer_ID, Volunteer_Name, Activity_Type, Duration_Hours, and Positive_Effects? Can you provide an example code snippet for this task? Assuming the HTML page looks like this:
Volunteer ID Volunteer Name Activity Type Duration Hours Positive Effects
1 John Teaching 4 Improved student performance
2 Jane Community service 3 Improved community involvement
3 Bob Mentoring 2 Improved self-esteem for mentee
You can use Nokogiri gem to extract data from the table and summarize it using the following code snippet: require 'nokogiri' require 'open-uri' doc = Nokogiri::HTML(open('path/to/html/file.html')) table = doc.css('table') volunteer_data = [] table.css('tr').each do |row| volunteer_data << { id: row.css('td')[0].text, name: row.css('td')[1].text, activity: row.css('td')[2].text, duration: row.css('td')[3].text, effect: row.css('td')[4].text } end summary = "Summary of The Benefits of Volunteering:\n\n" positive_effects = [] volunteer_data.each do |volunteer| positive_effects << volunteer[:effect] end summary << "The positive effects of volunteering include:\n" summary << "- " + positive_effects.join("\n- ") puts summary This code snippet extracts the data from the HTML table and stores it in an array of hashes. It then generates a summary of the positive effects of volunteering by iterating through the array and appending the effects to a string. Finally, it prints out the summary.

```python
wh = '\x1b[37m'
```

Your task is to determine the text color represented by the escape code `'\x1b[37m'` and write a function that can convert this code into a more human-readable format.

Write a function `get_text_color(escape_code: str) -> str` that takes an escape code as input and returns the corresponding text color as a string. The input escape code will always be in the format `\x1b[xxm`, where `xx` represents the color code. The output text color should be one of the following options: `'black'`, `'red'`, `'green'`, `'yellow'`, `'blue'`, `'magenta'`, `'cyan'`, or `'white'`. Any invalid or unsupported escape code should be mapped to an empty string (`''`).

**Examples:**

1. `get_text_color('\x1b[37m')` ➞ `'white'`
2. `get_text_color('\x1b[32m')` ➞ `'green'`
3. `get_text_color('\x1b[41m')` ➞ `''`

**Note:**

- The escape code `'\x1b[37m'` represents the text color white.
- The escape code `'\x1b[32m'` represents the text color green.
- The escape code `'\x1b[41m'` is not a valid text color.

prompt:
hello! I would like you're help building a slack app that sends job failure and success notifications. I found a lot of documentation on the topic, can I provide you this information over a series of messages? Here's the first instructions, below. When you are ready for more, respond OK.

Creating Slack Messages from Platform
Avatar Tori Becker
Updated 1 year ago
Part 1: Creating the Slack Credential 
Part 2: Creating the Platform Credential 
Part 3: Running the Platform Job 
Part 1: Creating the Slack Credential 
Click on the workspace dropdown in the top left of Slack, and select “Customize [Workspace name]”:

mceclip0.png

 

This will take you to the web browser of Slack. Click “Menu” in the top left corner, followed by “Configure apps”: 

mceclip1.png
 

That will take you to the Slack App Directory. Then click “Build” in the main toolbar:

mceclip2.png

 

Click “Create New App”. It's common for the app to have to be approved by the Slack administrator at your organization:

mceclip3.png


You’ll now be prompted to follow a series of set up steps. You should follow the Slack documentation to get to the level of access you’ll need for your given use case. Here are some help docs that helped us as we made this: 

https://api.slack.com/authentication/basics#calling
https://api.slack.com/messaging/composing/layouts#attachments
 
 

If you’re planning to use the Platform to Slack script template in part 3, you’ll need the Slack app to at least have the following features and functionalities added (screenshot below), as well as the specific OAuth scopes (Slack’s term for permissions), found under “Permissions”:

mceclip4.png

 

mceclip5.png

 

Note: the OAuth Scopes in Slack have great descriptions, as shown above. Use those to determine if you need to add additional scopes for your use case. You can also use incoming-webhooks to create messages from Google Forms, etc. 


Once the scopes have been added, it’s time to request your app be installed by your administrator. This will allow you to get the OAuth token you’ll need to make a Platform credential. You can do this in the “OAuth & Permissions” page of your app, click “Request to Install”: 

mceclip6.png

Once your admin approves of the app, Slack will alert you by sending a Slackbot message like this: 

mceclip7.png

Now, you should be able to see an OAuth token that’s been generated for your app. This is blocked out in the below screenshot for security purposes but you should see a long password value in this field that you can copy: 

mceclip8.png

Lastly, make sure to add the app to the channels you want it to write to in Slack. You can do so by opening the Slack channel details, clicking “Integrations”, and clicking “Add apps”: 

mceclip10.png

 

 

Part 2: Creating the Platform Credential 
Now, it’s time to store your Slack OAuth in Platform. You can follow these instructions for generic credential creation. 

 

“Name” will be what’s displayed in Platform, so pick something intuitive. 
“Type” should be “Custom”. 
“Username” can be anything, but cannot be blank. 
“Password” will be the OAuth Token displayed by Slack in the screenshot above. 
“Remote host” should be left blank. 
mceclip11.png

Part 3: Running the Platform Job 
 

Create a new script from this template. If you are not shared on template 178902, please contact support@civisanalytics.com . 

“Credential” should be the Platform credential created in step 2. 
“Channel” should be the Slack channel you wish to write to. 
“Text” should be the text you wish to post. For emojis, links, or mentions, please check this Slack documentation for the proper syntax i.e. :heavy\_check\_mark: for ✔️or for @here 
Run your job and expect the text to be posted in the designated channel like so:mceclip0.png

Pro tip: include this job at the end of a workflow.
